paras.text: This library uses a simple code on key slides in the format [Chapter] – [Sub-category] – [Number] to show where each activity sits in our training model.
The chapter (A, MP, EG, etc.) tells you the phase of the session, and the sub-category (e.g., PO, MT, FT, HP, TR+BP) links the exercise directly to the Belco Curriculum and Croatian principles.
You only need to code the first slide of each section — the system simply makes the library faster to navigate and easier to plan from.

TAKE BALL+PLAYER INTO THE NEXT PHASE
This activation practice will help the players lock-in on the nights theme of building through the thirds.
We look to get the ball + a player into the next phase/third of the attack.
The player can carry/dribble the ball into the next third.
The player can be passed into the next third.
The player can receive a bounce pass into the next third.
Once across you have an overload in the middle, use it, to get someone into the last third.
Need to get to the neutral player at the end and then turn and come back.
When possession is turned over, the new attacking team can play to an end and start their own buildup.
At all times it must be 3v2 in the square where the ball starts.
Players need to scan and be aware when to drop out of the area. Which will lead to some rotation and that’s ok.

This practice is the start of practicing winning the ball back in midfield.
Starts with a 3v1, and when the 1 wins it, they pass out of the square and then it becomes 5v3.
When ball is won back by blue they take it back in the square, and a new red goes in.
3v1 are trying to get to 10 passes, if they can. A new girls goes in.
Recover the ball back quickly is the theme. Your outnumbered and need to work out how to force them into a corner. Try dictate the moment.
Step in between passes, to cut options, angle your run to the ball carrier to force them wide.

This practice is the start of practicing winning the ball back in midfield.
Starts with a 3v1 or 4v2, and when the 1 wins it, they pass out of the square and then it becomes 5v3.
When ball is won back by blue they take it back in the square, and a new red goes in.
Start with the 4v2, when ball is won or goes out of square it becomes 7v4.
If the 4 get 8 passes, a new girl defends. If the 7 get 8 then it’s a goal.
BP>BPO, can we close the ball quickly.
BPO>BP, can we slow the tempo, and build passes.
Are we supporting, or just tired standing behind people.

6v6, directional game. Attacking team starts in their back half, and try keep possession  for 10 passes.
If reds win, they can score in goals in the half that they won the ball. If they score they become attacking team for next restart.
Blues should try win the ball back quickly, and then they get to continue attacking.
Reds can also make 4 passes to become the attacking team for next restart.

If defender touches ball but doesn’t win it, attackers need to play 3 one-touch passes.

7 or 8 v 4 on two fields. 25 x 36m. Blue plays out and tries to play through the 4 reds to end-zone. If they get through then they set up again with a back 4 and go the other way.
Reds are staying compact, protecting central areas and waiting for a trigger/queue to press.
Triggers are ball passed wide, bad touch, passes backward, too many touches/holds ball. Press aggressively on the trigger.
Reds can go to the mini goals quickly after winning ball, then blue restarts.

5V5 and practicing pushing a player forward in attack, and dropping a player when defending.
In attack, teams play 2-3, when defending it’s 3-2.
In this SSG any player can help the attack, any can help the defence.
Goals are scored when the ball is controlled over the end line.
Keep your shape, think about distances between the lines.
Think about covering your first defender.
Think about do we need the spare defender, do we want to press and go 1 for 1.
Team tries to dribble into end zone to score. If they get there, turn around and go the other way.
On the turnover the keep directions until a team scores.
Ask players to focus on how they support.
Are they opening passing lanes or blocking them? (are they standing in front of a teammate)
Are they able to progress the play if they receive? (level and outside)
Can they see both end zones? (body position)

Then players need to recognize the moment, and take it.

PLAYING OUT FROM THE BACK FUNDAMENTALS
Playing out works by three main fundamentals.
Passing options (you need many passing options in order to keep the ball or you are easily marked)
Overloads (you need to be able to recognize overload moments, make overload moments and take advantage of overload moments.

Forward passes and forward movement (you can pass all day but if it doesn’t get you forwards, there’s no real purpose – unless you’re winning and killing the game. You need to play forward passes and then have forward movement to progress the attack phases.

Wait till they play one way to know which midfielder to push on.

Win the ball and go to goal as quickly as possible.

This session is practicing how to defend the counter attack. Working on the priorities for the first defender, and also the priorities of the second and this defenders and the midfielders and their recovery runs.
Emphasis is first on the first defender, and their tracking run, and their ability to delay.
Don’t run straight at defender, run more to goal and then bend to attacker.
Don’t jump in to try win the ball, because will assist them to make quick decisions because that will help their counter. Delay, slow them down, show them away from goal, be patient.
Allow your team mates more time for their recovery runs to assist. Make sure to keep an eye on supporting attackers, cover them.

Coaching messages are that when we play out, once we go one way, that side of the diamond gives some width.
Try to play the straight ball to the 8, which help bounce the fullback out.
Don’t need the CBs stepping in, that area will likely already be crowded, width is where the space is.
When the full back is driving, if in open-ball, you can switch in behind to the second striker.
Otherwise play the 9, and they can bounce the 10 in facing forwards.
